subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! Look around you and you will notice that patterns are everywhere — in the tiles on a masjid floor, in the petals of a flower, in the way market prices climb by a steady amount, and in the neat rows of a football stadium. In this unit you will learn to describe these patterns using the language of mathematics. You will study sequences of numbers, discover how to jump straight to any term you like using a rule, and meet several families of special numbers: square numbers, cube numbers, triangular numbers, prime numbers, and the factors and multiples that connect them all.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 6 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Generate sequences using term-to-term rules (adding or subtracting a constant), including decimals and decreasing sequences.
Find any term in a sequence using a position-to-term (nth term) rule, without listing every term.
Use flow diagrams and stopping conditions to build sequences step by step.
Calculate and recognise square numbers up to at least 12² and cube numbers up to at least 6³.
Compare and order square and cube numbers, and expressions such as 2⁴ and 3².
Find multiples and common multiples of two or more numbers, including the lowest common multiple (LCM).
Find factors and common factors of numbers, including the highest common factor (HCF), and identify prime numbers.
Investigate special patterns: triangular numbers, hexagonal numbers, consecutive square sums, and consecutive odd sums.
Solve real-world problems using sequences, multiples and factors, and justify your reasoning.

concepts.title: Sequences, Terms and Positions
concepts.explanation: A sequence is a list of numbers that follows a pattern or rule. Each number in the sequence is called a term, and the place a term sits in is called its position. Consider the sequence 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 … Here the 1st term is 2, the 2nd term is 4, the 3rd term is 6, and so on. Keeping the position and the term clearly separate is the single most important habit in this whole topic. A very common slip is to confuse the position number with the term value. In the sequence 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, the number in position 5 is…
concepts.example: Sequences appear constantly in everyday life. When Khadija stacks bariis (rice) sacks in a store — 20 in the first row, 22 in the second, 24 in the third — she is building a sequence. When Yusuf watches the number of…

concepts.title: Term-to-Term Rules
concepts.explanation: A term-to-term rule tells you how to get from one term to the next term. In the sequence 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 …, the term-to-term rule is add 2: 2 + 2 = 4, then 4 + 2 = 6, then 6 + 2 = 8, and so on. The amount you add (or subtract) each time is called the common difference, and it stays the same all the way through the sequence. Sequences can step up by different amounts. Counting in 4s gives 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 …, where the term-to-term rule is add 4. Sequences can also involve decimals. The sequence 0.25, 0.5, 0.75,…
concepts.example: Not every sequence grows. Some decrease. Take 15, 14.6, 14.2, 13.8, 13.4 … Here the term-to-term rule is subtract 0.4: you start at 15 and count back by 0.4 each time. To find the term-to-term rule of any sequence, look…

concepts.title: Position-to-Term (nth Term) Rules
concepts.explanation: A position-to-term rule — often called the nth term rule — lets you find any term directly from its position, without listing all the terms before it. Look again at 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 … Notice that position 1 gives 1 × 2 = 2, position 2 gives 2 × 2 = 4, and position 3 gives 3 × 2 = 6. The rule is “multiply the position by 2,” which we can write as nth term = 2n, where n stands for the position. This is enormously powerful. Once you know the rule is 2n, you can leap straight to any term: the 25th term is 25 × 2 =…
concepts.example: Many nth term rules have two parts: a multiply and then an add or subtract. Consider the sequence 5, 8, 11, 14, 17 … The terms go up by 3 each time, so the rule contains 3n. But 3 × 1 = 3, which is 2 less than the first…

concepts.title: Flow Diagrams and Stopping Conditions
concepts.explanation: A flow diagram is a systematic set of instructions for building a sequence one step at a time. It usually has a starting number, an operation to repeat, and a stopping condition that tells you when to finish. Flow diagrams are especially useful when a sequence is built by more than one operation, or when it must stop once the numbers pass a certain size. Here is a simple flow diagram in words: start with 2 → add 4 → is the answer more than 20? If no, write it and add 4 again. If yes, stop. Following it gives 2, 6,…
concepts.example: Flow diagrams can combine operations. Consider start with 2 → multiply by 3 → subtract 1 → repeat until the result is greater than 50. Starting at 2: 2 × 3 − 1 = 5, then 5 × 3 − 1 = 14, then 14 × 3 − 1 = 41, then 41 × 3…

concepts.title: Square Numbers
concepts.explanation: A square number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself. We write “5 squared” as 5², which means 5 × 5 = 25. The small raised 2 is called an index or power, and it tells you how many times to use the number in the multiplication. The first few square numbers are 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144 — that is 1² up to 12². Why are they called *square* numbers? Because you can arrange exactly that many dots into a perfect square. 1² is a single dot. 2² = 4 dots make a 2-by-2 square. 3² =…
concepts.example: There is a lovely hidden pattern in the square numbers. Look at the differences between consecutive squares: 4 − 1 = 3, 9 − 4 = 5, 16 − 9 = 7, 25 − 16 = 9. The differences are the odd numbers 3, 5, 7, 9 …! This is…

concepts.title: Cube Numbers and Comparing Powers
concepts.explanation: A cube number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself, and then by itself again — three of the same number multiplied together. We write “2 cubed” as 2³, which means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8. The first few cube numbers are 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216 — that is 1³ up to 6³. Cube numbers grow much faster than square numbers because you are multiplying three times, not two. The name *cube* comes from a 3-D picture, just as *square* came from a flat one. Think of small building blocks stacked into a solid cube. A…
concepts.example: A key skill is comparing different powers to decide which is larger. Never assume the bigger-looking expression wins — always work each one out fully. Compare 2⁴ and 3²: here 2⁴ = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 16, while 3² = 3 × 3 =…

methods.title: How to find the nth term rule
methods.example: For the sequence 7, 12, 17, 22 …, find the next term, the term-to-term rule, and the nth term rule.
steps: Find the common difference between terms — this is the multiplier of n.
Multiply the position 1 by that difference.
Compare with the actual first term, and add or subtract to make them match.
Check your rule on the 3rd or 4th term.

methods.title: How to find the LCM of two numbers
methods.example: A cinema has 15 seats in row 1, 18 in row 2, 21 in row 3, and so on. How many seats are in row 8?
steps: List the multiples of each number in order.
Look for the first value that appears in both lists.
That value is the lowest common multiple.

methods.title: How to find the HCF of two numbers
methods.example: Start with 3 → multiply by 2 → add 5 → is the result more than 20? Show the first result and whether you continue.
steps: List all the factors of each number, working in pairs.
Circle the factors that appear in both lists (the common factors).
The largest circled factor is the highest common factor.

methods.title: Square and cube calculations
methods.example: Calculate 8², 4³, and decide which is larger: 3⁴ or 2⁵.
steps: Solution: 8² = 8 × 8 = 64.
4³ = 4 × 4 × 4 = 64 (interestingly, the same value).
For the comparison, 3⁴ = 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81 and 2⁵ = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 32, so 3⁴ > 2⁵ because 81 is greater than 32.

methods.title: Lowest common multiple
methods.example: Bus A comes every 12 minutes and Bus B every 15 minutes. Both arrive at 2:00 PM. When do they next arrive together?
steps: Solution: Find the LCM of 12 and 15.
Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, 72 … Multiples of 15: 15, 30, 45, 60, 75 … The smallest shared value is 60, so they arrive together every 60 minutes.
That is 3:00 PM, one hour after 2:00 PM.

methods.title: Highest common factor
steps: Solution: Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18.
The common factors are 1, 2, 3 and 6.
The largest is 6, so the HCF of 18 and 30 is 6.

workedExamples.title: Continue a sequence and find its rule
workedExamples.prompt: For the sequence 7, 12, 17, 22 …, find the next term, the term-to-term rule, and the nth term rule.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The gap between terms is 5 each time (12 − 7 = 5, 17 − 12 = 5), so the term-to-term rule is add 5 and the next term is 22 + 5 = 27. For the nth term rule, the common difference is 5, so it contains 5n. But 5 × 1 = 5, which is 2 more than the first term 7 … wait, 5 is 2 *less* than 7, so we add 2: the rule is nth term = 5n + 2. Check: position 3 gives 5 × 3 + 2 = 17. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Find a far-off term
workedExamples.prompt: A cinema has 15 seats in row 1, 18 in row 2, 21 in row 3, and so on. How many seats are in row 8?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The seats go up by 3 each row, starting at 15. To reach row 8 from row 1 we add 3 a total of 7 times (there are 7 gaps between row 1 and row 8). So row 8 = 15 + 7 × 3 = 15 + 21 = 36 seats. Counting the gaps, not the rows, is the key idea here.

workedExamples.title: Follow a flow diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Start with 3 → multiply by 2 → add 5 → is the result more than 20? Show the first result and whether you continue.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 3 × 2 = 6, then 6 + 5 = 11. Is 11 more than 20? No, so you continue. The first result written down is 11.

workedExamples.title: Square and cube calculations
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate 8², 4³, and decide which is larger: 3⁴ or 2⁵.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 8² = 8 × 8 = 64. 4³ = 4 × 4 × 4 = 64 (interestingly, the same value). For the comparison, 3⁴ = 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81 and 2⁵ = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 32, so 3⁴ > 2⁵ because 81 is greater than 32.

workedExamples.title: Lowest common multiple
workedExamples.prompt: Bus A comes every 12 minutes and Bus B every 15 minutes. Both arrive at 2:00 PM. When do they next arrive together?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the LCM of 12 and 15. Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, 72 … Multiples of 15: 15, 30, 45, 60, 75 … The smallest shared value is 60, so they arrive together every 60 minutes. That is 3:00 PM, one hour after 2:00 PM.

workedExamples.title: Highest common factor
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18. Factors of 30: 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30. The common factors are 1, 2, 3 and 6. The largest is 6, so the HCF of 18 and 30 is 6.

workedExamples.title: Counting all factors
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Work in pairs: 1 × 36, 2 × 18, 3 × 12, 4 × 9, 6 × 6. The 6 repeats itself, so we count it once. The factors are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36 — that is 9 factors. (Square numbers always have an odd number of factors, because one factor pairs with itself.)

workedExamples.title: A square-formation problem
workedExamples.prompt: A school wants to arrange 144 chairs in a perfect square formation. How many rows are needed?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A square formation needs a number of rows whose square is 144. Since 12 × 12 = 144, we have 144 = 12², so there must be 12 rows of 12 chairs. Recognising 144 as a square number solves it instantly.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find the highest common factor (HCF) of: a) 12 and 20 b) 18 and 30.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Complete the differences between consecutive squares: 3² − 2² = ___, 4² − 3² = ___, 5² − 4² = ___. What do you notice?
workedExamples.solution: 5, 7, 9 — the differences are consecutive odd numbers.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the flow diagram from 1: start → ×5 → +2 → ÷3 → result. Show each step.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find two consecutive square numbers that differ by 17.
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Build the Square and Cube Numbers
activities.materials: stones, dry beans, or bottle caps, and a flat surface.
steps: Arrange objects into squares: 1, then a 2×2 (4), then a 3×3 (9), then a 4×4 (16). Say each square number aloud.
Count the objects each time and write the square number beside your picture.
Now stack small cubes or boxes to build 2×2×2 (8) and 3×3×3 (27) cubes if you can.
Explain to a family member why 9 is called a square number and 8 is called a cube number.
Keep a chart of your square and cube numbers — you can use it all through this unit.

activities.title: The Factor Sharing Game
activities.materials: 24 small objects (dates, stones, or counters) and some friends or family.
steps: Try to share the 24 objects equally among 2 people, then 3, then 4, then 5, then 6.
Each time, note whether it works with nothing left over.
The group sizes that work are the factors of 24. Write them all down.
Repeat with 36 objects and compare which factors 24 and 36 share (their common factors).

activities.title: Common Multiple Clap Game
activities.materials: just you and one other person (or count in your head).
steps: One player claps on every multiple of 3; the other stamps on every multiple of 4.
Count together: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 … up to 24.
Listen for the numbers where a clap and a stamp happen together — these are common multiples of 3 and 4.
The first time they happen together (12) is the lowest common multiple. Try new pairs like 2 and 5.
This game trains you to hear common multiples, not just calculate them.

activities.title: Sequence Snake Investigation
steps: Draw a long snake and divide its body into boxes.
Choose a starting number and a rule (for example, start at 5, add 7 each time).
Fill the snake with your sequence, then work out its nth term rule.
Give a friend a snake with some boxes blank and challenge them to fill the gaps and find your rule.

activities.title: The Prime Number Hunt (Sieve)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a coloured pencil.
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 50 in a grid, ten to a row.
Cross out 1 (it is not prime). Circle 2, then cross out every other multiple of 2.
Circle 3, then cross out every other multiple of 3. Do the same for 5 and 7.
The numbers still circled are the prime numbers up to 50. List them and check you have them all.
This method is called the Sieve of Eratosthenes, named after an ancient mathematician.

activities.title: Triangular Numbers with the Family
activities.materials: stones or coins and your family or friends.
steps: Arrange 1 stone, then a triangle of 3, then 6, then 10, then 15. These are the triangular numbers.
Notice how many stones you add each time: 2, then 3, then 4, then 5.
Ask a family member to predict the next triangular number before you build it.
Discuss where triangular patterns appear in real life, such as stacked oranges or bowling pins.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand factors and multiples best? What surprised you about square, cube or prime numbers? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Welcome to Unit 2! Look around you and you will notice that patterns are everywhere — in the tiles on a masjid floor, in the petals of a flower, in the way market prices climb by a steady amount, and in the neat rows of a football stadium. In this unit you will learn to describe these patterns using the language of mathematics. You will study sequences of numbers, discover how to jump straight to any term you like using a rule, and meet several families of special numbers: square numbers, cube numbers, triangular numbers, prime numbers, and the factors and multiples that connect them all.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: By the end of this unit you will be able to generate and continue sequences, find any term using a position-to-term rule (also called an nth term rule), calculate and recognise square and cube numbers, find common multiples and common factors of two numbers, and investigate beautiful patterns such as the link between odd numbers and square numbers. These ideas are the foundation of the algebra, geometry and number theory you will meet in the years ahead, so time spent here is time very well spent.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: Find any term in a sequence using a position-to-term (nth term) rule, without listing every term.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: A list of numbers that follows a rule

rules.title: Key idea 5
rules.text: A rule to find any term from its position

rules.title: Key idea 6
rules.text: A sequence is a list of numbers that follows a pattern or rule. Each number in the sequence is called a term, and the place a term sits in is called its position. Consider the sequence 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 … Here the 1st term is 2, the 2nd term is 4, the 3rd term is 6, and so on. Keeping the position and the term clearly separate is the single most important habit in this whole topic.

terms: A patterned list / one number in it
How to get from one term to the next
Find any term from its position
Lowest common multiple / highest common factor
commonMistakes: Factors divide in; multiples are built up
A prime needs exactly two factors
LCM = smallest shared multiple; HCF = largest shared factor

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Cube Numbers and Comparing Powers.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A cube number is what you get when you multiply a whole number by itself, and then by itself again — three of the same number multiplied together. We write “2 cubed” as 2³, which means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8. The first few cube numbers are 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216 — that is 1³ up to 6³. Cube numbers grow much faster than square numbers because you are multiplying three times, not two. The name *cube* comes from a 3-D picture, just as *square* came from a flat one. Think of small building blocks stacked into a solid cube. A…

selfAssessment: I can generate sequences using term-to-term rules (adding or subtracting a constant), including decimals and decreasing sequences.
I can find any term in a sequence using a position-to-term (nth term) rule, without listing every term.
I can use flow diagrams and stopping conditions to build sequences step by step.
I can calculate and recognise square numbers up to at least 12² and cube numbers up to at least 6³.
I can compare and order square and cube numbers, and expressions such as 2⁴ and 3².
I can find multiples and common multiples of two or more numbers, including the lowest common multiple (LCM).
I can find factors and common factors of numbers, including the highest common factor (HCF), and identify prime numbers.
I can investigate special patterns: triangular numbers, hexagonal numbers, consecutive square sums, and consecutive odd sums.
